metadata.title: Short-term fasting abolishes the sex-related difference in GH and leptin secretion in humans
metadata.study_design: Prospective controlled study with repeated measures
metadata.demographics: Fifteen normal adult volunteers: eight men (age 29.3 ± 1.2 yr, BMI 22.2 ± 0.5 kg/m²) and seven women (age 28.0 ± 0.8 yr, BMI 20.1 ± 0.7 kg/m²). All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase.

evidence.quote: Short-term fasting abolishes the sex-related difference in GH and leptin secretion in humans

We studied growth hormone (GH) and leptin secretion in eight male (age 29.3 6 1.2 yr, body mass index 22.2 6 0.5 kg/m2) and seven female normal subjects (28.0 6 0.8 yr, 20.1 6 0.7 kg/m2) before and after 36 h of fasting
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 279: E411–E416, 2000
All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase
eight male (age 29.3 6 1.2 yr, body mass index 22.2 6 0.5 kg/m2)
All subjects had had stable weight in the last 6 mo before entering the study protocol

methods_participants.label: Diet composition - Carbohydrates
methods_participants.value: Classical Mediterranean diet (~50% carbohydrates, 30% lipids, 20% proteins)

evidence.quote: were on a classical Mediterranean diet (;50% carbohydrates, 30% lipids, and 20% proteins)
before and after 36 h of fasting
From 8 AM to 4 PM, blood samples were taken every 30 min
Serum insulin levels (mU/ml) were measured in duplicate by the RIA method (INSIK-5, Sorin)
The limit of sensitivity was 0.5 mg/l

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way ANOVA with between-subject (gender) and within-subject (fasting) factors

evidence.quote: The statistical analysis of the data was performed by a two-way ANOVA: between-subject (gender) and within-subject (fasting) factors and their interaction were considered
all subjects had a 200-kcal breakfast (60% carbohydrate) at 8 AM

narrative_results.statement: Females showed a higher leptin decrease than males after fasting

evidence.quote: Mean leptin concentration in men (2.7 ± 0.5 mg/l) was lower (P < 0.0001) than in women (5.9 ± 0.5 mg/l; Figs. 2-3).

narrative_results.statement: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes

evidence.quote: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.

narrative_results.statement: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased independently of gender

evidence.quote: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased (P < 0.001) independently of gender, whereas IGFBP-3, ALS, and GHBP were unchanged (Fig. 1).

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes GH assay sensitivity for IRMA while Pass 2 describes IGF-I assay method - completely different assays and parameters
Pass 1 describes GH assay sensitivity for IFA while Pass 2 describes Glucose assay method - completely different assays and parameters
Pass 1 describes Leptin assay sensitivity while Pass 2 describes Insulin assay method - completely different assays and parameters
Pass 1 states men had lower GH than women; Pass 2 states females showed lower GH increase than males after fasting. These describe opposite directional relationships.
Pass 1 states gender differences disappear after fasting; Pass 2 states women have higher GH than men in fed condition. These address different conditions and are incompatible.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Mean leptin concentration in men was lower than in women
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Mean leptin concentration in men was lower than in women before fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: When assayed by IRMA, mean GH concentration in men (0.5 ± 0.4 mg/l) was lower (P < 0.05) than in women (2.3 ± 0.4 mg/l).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes. There was significant interaction between gender and fasting (P < 0.05 for GH and P < 0.005 for leptin).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: When assayed by IRMA, mean GH concentration in men was lower than in women
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes. There was significant interaction between gender and fasting (P < 0.05 for GH and P < 0.005 for leptin).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men before any decrease in total IGF-I, as well as in IGFBP-3 and ALS levels.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men before any decrease in total IGF-I, as well as in IGFBP-3 and ALS levels.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: After fasting, gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels are no longer detectable

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In fact, after-fasting mean GH concentration values in females and males were similar.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a lower GH increase (P < 0.05 in terms of percent increase above baseline) than males independently of the assay used (males vs. females: IRMA, 6.2 ± 1.1 vs. 5.4 ± 1.2 mg/l; IFA, 3.3 ± 0.7 vs. 2.7 ± 0.5 mg/l).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: After fasting, mean GH and leptin concentration values in females and males were similar
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a lower GH increase than males after fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: On the other hand, females showed a higher leptin decrease (P < 0.05 in terms of percent decrease below baseline) than males (males vs. females: 0.3 ± 0.2 vs. 3.2 ± 0.5 mg/l, P < 0.001).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a higher leptin decrease than males after fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: On the other hand, females showed a higher leptin decrease (P < 0.05 in terms of percent decrease below baseline) than males (males vs. females: 0.3 ± 0.2 vs. 3.2 ± 0.5 mg/l, P < 0.001).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The results of the present study show that mean GH and leptin concentrations are higher in women than in men in the fed but not in the fasting condition.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Females showed a higher leptin decrease than males after fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Mean GH concentration in women is higher than in men in the fed condition

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 provides complete demographic details (age, BMI) while Pass 2 only states female count. Both confirm 7 females; Pass 1 is more comprehensive but same core fact.
Both describe weight stability requirement over 6 months. Pass 1 provides full context, Pass 2 is abbreviated but conveys identical information.
Both identify Mediterranean diet as inclusion criterion. Pass 1 includes macronutrient breakdown (~50/30/20), Pass 2 omits this detail. Same underlying fact with different specificity.
Both reference early follicular phase menstrual timing. Pass 1 adds context about regular menses requirement; Pass 2 focuses on phase timing. Compatible facts with different emphasis.
Both state 36-hour fasting duration. Identical fact with minor formatting differences in presentation.
Both reference 30-minute blood sampling intervals. Pass 1 provides full temporal context (8 AM to 4 PM), Pass 2 extracts only frequency value. Same underlying sampling protocol.
Both state 200 kcal breakfast. Pass 1 includes carbohydrate composition (60%) and timing (8 AM), Pass 2 extracts caloric value only. Same core fact with different detail levels.
Both state 800 kcal lunch. Pass 1 includes macronutrient breakdown (50/30/20), Pass 2 extracts caloric value only. Same meal specification with different granularity.
Both describe GH assay method 1 as IRMA/Immunoradiometric assay with identical meaning, only abbreviation vs full name difference
Both describe GH assay method 2 as IFA/Immunofunctional assay with identical meaning, only abbreviation vs full name difference
Pass 1 describes GH assay sensitivity for IRMA while Pass 2 describes IGF-I assay method - completely different assays and parameters
Pass 1 describes GH assay sensitivity for IFA while Pass 2 describes Glucose assay method - completely different assays and parameters
Both describe Leptin assay method as RIA/Radioimmunoassay with identical meaning, only abbreviation vs full name difference
Pass 1 describes Leptin assay sensitivity while Pass 2 describes Insulin assay method - completely different assays and parameters
Pass 1 contains Insulin assay method information but Pass 2 is empty - orphan entry
Pass 1 contains Insulin assay sensitivity information but Pass 2 is empty - orphan entry
Pass 1 contains glucose assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGF-I assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGF-I assay sensitivity information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-3 assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-3 assay sensitivity information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains ALS assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains GHBP assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-1 assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-1 assay sensitivity data; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains FFA assay method data; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Estradiol assay method data; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Estradiol assay sensitivity data; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Testosterone assay method data; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Testosterone assay sensitivity data; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Statistical analysis method data; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe 8 males; Pass 1 includes additional demographic details (age, BMI) while Pass 2 provides only count
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains male participant age data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains female participant age data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains male participant BMI data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains female participant BMI data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains blood sampling duration data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 states men had lower GH than women; Pass 2 states females showed lower GH increase than males after fasting. These describe opposite directional relationships.
Both extractions describe identical findings: fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes.
Same fact with negligible wording difference: 'sex-related' vs 'gender-specific' are synonymous descriptors.
Both state that after fasting, gender-related differences in GH and leptin are eliminated or similar between sexes. Wording differs but meaning is identical.
Pass 1 states gender differences disappear after fasting; Pass 2 states women have higher GH than men in fed condition. These address different conditions and are incompatible.
Both extractions are identical regarding IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increases after 36h fasting independent of gender.
Same fact with minor wording variation: 'sex-related fasting response' vs 'gender difference in fasting response' convey the same finding.
Essentially equivalent; Pass 2 adds temporal specificity ('before fasting') which clarifies but doesn't contradict Pass 1's finding.
